narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as a tough one—72% chance of not passing, based on patterns from thousands of students like you. Picture your journey starting with that Year 1 decile of 3, which placed you solidly in the middle of the pack but signaled to the model early on that building momentum might be a challenge, pulling strongly toward the risk of failure. By Year 3, your decile slipped to 4, becoming the biggest red flag of all—it was the strongest influence, whispering that even with time, your grades weren't climbing into the top tiers where bar success stories thrive. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 23, which stood out as the second-most powerful factor pushing toward failure, as it fell short of the benchmarks that typically light the way to passing. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.2 added to that narrative, suggesting a steady but not standout academic foundation, while your middle family income bracket hinted at fewer resources for the intense prep that often makes the difference. As an Asian female studying full-time, these details together painted a picture the model has seen before—one where the odds stack up against crossing the bar finish line without extraordinary shifts. It's not about your potential or drive; it's the model's honest read on how these pieces fit past patterns. The good news? You're holding all the cards to rewrite this story—target a law school that matches your trajectory, amp up those study habits, and maybe seek extra bar prep support early. You've got this spark; now channel it to prove the model wrong. Keep pushing forward.
